Title: More than shells on the shore: RM7.3mil cocaine found on beach
Article:
KUANTAN: Police are investigating the discovery of suspected cocaine weighing more than 16kg, with an estimated street value of RM7.31 million, which washed ashore on a beach in Pekan.
Pahang police chief Datuk Seri Yahaya Othman said officers were alerted by members of the public to a suspicious bag found at Pantai Tanjung Gosong, Tanjung Selangor, at 2.45pm yesterday.
A subsequent inspection uncovered 14 compressed black packages labelled "stone", believed to contain cocaine, weighing a total of 16.263kg and still in good condition.
Six additional packages bearing the same marking were also found, but their contents had dissolved, likely due to prolonged exposure to seawater.
"The estimated market value of the seized cocaine is RM7.31 million, based on a local price of around RM450,000 per kilogram," he told a press conference at the Pahang police headquarters.
Police said the quantity seized could potentially affect more than 81,000 users.
Investigations are ongoing, with the state's narcotics criminal investigation department conducting further forensic examinations and recording witness statements to identify those involved.
No arrests have been made so far, and authorities are also trying to determine how long the bag had been at sea before it was discovered.
Yahaya described the case as rare and urged anyone with information or video footage related to the incident to come forward.
Members of the public can contact investigating officer Inspector Khairul Azhar Ahmad at 09-42212222 or the narcotics hotline at 012-2087222.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: HRPB Cardiology team makes history again, wins ACC 2026 world title
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Cardiology team from Hospital Raja Permaisuri Bainun (HRPB), Ipoh has once again brought pride to Malaysia by emerging as world champions in the Fellows-in-Training (FITs) Jeopardy competition at the American College of Cardiology (ACC) Conference 2026 in New Orleans.
The achievement was secured through the combined efforts of three young Malaysian specialists, Dr Chen Tai Meng, Dr Ang Jian-Gang and Dr Yee Shen Yew, who outperformed participants from various countries.
Health Minister Datuk Seri Dr Dzulkefly Ahmad said the prestigious accomplishment proved that Malaysian medical specialists were on par with the best at the international level.
"Congratulations also to Dr Nor Hanim Mohd Amin (Head of HRPB'S Cardiology Department) and the entire HRPB Cardiology team for their strong leadership and support.
"This success marks an extraordinary 'hat-trick' of excellence after winning the national title organised by the National Heart Association of Malaysia in 2024 and the regional ACC Asia title in Singapore in 2025," he said in a post on X today.
Held from March 28 to 30, the FITs Jeopardy is a prestigious interactive learning platform that combines elements of quizzes, competition and education, specifically for cardiology trainees to enhance their knowledge and build global professional networks. – BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Apex court dismisses NGO's appeal over Bukit Cherakah degazettement
Article:
PUTRAJAYA: The Federal Court, in a 2-1 majority decision, dismissed an appeal by two non-governmental organisations (NGOs) challenging the degazettement of Bukit Cherakah as a forest reserve.
The two NGOs are Pertubuhan Pelindung Khazanah Alam Malaysia (Peka) and the Shah Alam Community Forest Society (SACF).
Court of Appeal President Datuk Seri Abu Bakar Jais and Federal Court Judge Datuk Rhodzariah Bujang formed the majority in dismissing the appeal, while Federal Court Judge Datuk Lee Swee Seng delivered a dissenting judgment.
The court made no order as to costs.
On Sept 9, 2024, the apex court granted the two NGOs leave to appeal against the dismissal of their judicial review application challenging the matter.
In 2022, the Shah Alam High Court denied the NGOs leave to commence a judicial review of the Selangor government's decision to degazette 406.22ha of Bukit Cherakah.
Their subsequent appeal was dismissed by the Court of Appeal on Jan 24, 2024, on the grounds that the judicial review application had been filed out of time.
The NGOs then took the matter to the Federal Court, seeking leave to appeal the lower court's decision, claiming it was illegal for the Selangor government to backdate a gazette, stating that the degazettement decision was made on Nov 20, 2000.
They said the gazette was published on May 5, 2022, almost 22 years after the decision to degazette Bukit Cherakah.
In their application for judicial review, the NGOs named the Selangor government, the State Executive Council, the State Forestry Department director, and the Petaling Land and Mines Department as respondents.
The other respondents were YCH Development Sdn Bhd, Perbadanan Kemajuan Negeri Selangor and Restu Mantap Sdn Bhd.
The NGOs were represented by lawyers Datuk Gurdial Singh Nijar, Rajesh Nagarajan and Amanda Sonia Mathew, while Assistant state legal advisor Hani Aziza Ismail@ Aziz and Mary Phoon Keat Mee appeared for the Selangor government, the State Executive Council, the State Forestry Department director, and the Petaling Land and Mines Department.
The other lawyers were Ng Ka Choon and Low Jiah Yee for YCH Development Sdn Bhd, Ganapathi Ramasamy, Al Sabri Ahmad Kabri and Marlia Mohd Azrin for Perbadanan Kemajuan Negeri Selangor, and John Wong Yok Hon for Restu Mantap Sdn Bhd.
Lawyers New Sin Yew and Abhilaash Subramaniam represented the Malaysian Bar as amici curiae (friends of the court). – BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Court defers proceedings in murder case against Nepali for interpreter
Article:
BUKIT MERTAJAM: The magistrate's court here today deferred proceedings against a Nepali man charged with murdering his co-worker to allow for the appointment of an interpreter.
Magistrate Nurul Rasyidah Mohd Akit set April 21 for the appointment to ensure the charge against the accused, Dangi Chopilal, 24, is properly read and explained to him.
No bail was granted.
Dangi is supposed to have faced a charge of murdering his 22-year-old compatriot in the compound of a factory in Permatang Tinggi on March 22.
He is supposed to be charged under Section 302 of the Penal Code, which carries the death penalty upon conviction.
It was reported that the suspect, a worker at a duck processing factory, was remanded to assist investigations into the case.
In the 1.10am incident, the victim sustained fatal stab wounds and was later pronounced dead while receiving treatment at Bukit Mertajam Hospital.
Investigations indicate that both men, who work at the same factory, are believed to have been involved in a verbal altercation at their hostel prior to the incident.
The victim had later gone out for a meal and, upon returning, was allegedly
attacked by the suspect while relieving himself
.
Preliminary findings revealed stab wounds to the victim's ribs.
Police later detained the suspect at the workers' hostel and seized a knife believed to have been used in the attack.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Index crime drops during Hari Raya, say police
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Index crime declined during the Hari Raya Aidilfitri period, says Federal police Crime Prevention and Community Safety Department director Datuk Seri Mohd Yusri Hassan Basri.
He said cases fell by 111, from 858 during the corresponding period in 2025 to 747 this year, with the improvement accompanied by increased public participation through the Volunteer Smartphone Patrol (VSP) application under Op Selamat 26.
"Comparisons between Op Selamat 24, held from March 29 to April 3, 2025, and Op Selamat 26, from March 19 to 24 this year, showed a rise in the use of the VSP complaint module from 80 cases to 150.
"The 'balik kampung' notification module also recorded a significant increase from 8,036 to 16,889, while use of the emergency panic button feature rose from five to seven cases," he said.
Yusri added that Op Selamat 26 recorded 61,122 visits to unattended homes and 31,636 mobile patrol vehicle operations, compared with Op Selamat 24, which saw 76,274 visits and 37,429 patrols carried out.
"The positive trend in the crime index allowed for more focused and comprehensive patrols and visits to unattended homes," he said.
As of March 30, VSP membership had reached 1,705,852 users, reflecting growing public awareness and commitment to working with the police in crime prevention.
"We will continue to strengthen crime prevention strategies through patrols, enforcement and community engagement. This synergy is expected to further enhance national safety and well-being," he added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: WHO chief praises Malaysia's role in strengthening global health cooperation
Article:
PUTRAJAYA: The Director-General of the World Health Organisation, Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, has praised Malaysia for its commitment and active engagement in international health issues, as well as its role in strengthening global cooperation.
Malaysia's Health Ministry said the acknowledgement came during Dr Tedros' official visit, reflecting what it described as a close and sustained partnership between Malaysia and the WHO in enhancing health system preparedness and resilience.
The ministry said the visit has direct implications for Malaysians, particularly in improving prevention, preparedness and public health response through ongoing negotiations on pathogen access and benefit sharing under the Intergovernmental Working Group.
It added that the collaboration would strengthen national preparedness and early warning systems against infectious disease threats, including through amendments to the International Health Regulations and the proposed pandemic agreement.
"These efforts will enable Malaysia to detect and respond more swiftly to future health crises, while minimising disruptions to economic activity and social continuity," the statement said.
Meanwhile, Health Minister Datuk Seri Dr Dzulkefly Ahmad said Malaysia's active participation in the WHO ensures the country's voice remains represented in the global health landscape.
He added that Malaysia remains committed to working closely with the organisation to ensure its healthcare system stays robust, inclusive and prepared for future global health challenges.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Widow seeks 'justice' as husband's alleged drunk driver charged with murder
Article:
KLANG: Although the court proceedings lasted just over five minutes, seeing the man accused of killing her husband formally charged with murder gave Nor Nadia Abdul Majid a sense of justice.
The 32-year-old mother of three appeared composed in the public gallery as the interpreter read out the charge.
Seated in the second row, she was only a few steps from the accused, R. Saktygaanapathy, 28, who stood in the dock.
After the proceedings, Nadia became tearful as officers from the Klang Selatan Traffic Enforcement and Investigation Department spoke with her.
"I am grateful that he has been charged. Alhamdulillah, I feel relieved. I only have one thing to say… an eye for an eye. That is all I seek," she said outside the courtroom.
Nadia, whose husband Amirul Hafiz Omar, 32, was buried just two days ago, said she is preparing to return to her hometown in Taiping, Perak.
"It was my late husband's wish that if anything happened, I would move back to my kampung, since we don't have many family members here.
"We lived in Shah Alam with only a few relatives nearby. I came back to handle school transfers for my two older children.
"I have more family in Taiping who can help care for my three children when I am busy," she added.
Nadia, who works part-time as a parcel delivery worker, said her children — Muhammad Aqif Amsyar, nine; Muhammad Afif Izz Rayqal, seven; and Nur Humaira, two — deeply miss their father and sleep with his clothes to feel close to him.
Her relatives had also brought Nur Humaira to his grave.
"She has never been to a grave before. She said, 'Papa is inside,'" Nadia recalled.
"I hope my children stay strong and receive emotional support. After losing my husband, the last thing I want is for my eldest son to feel guilty for asking his father to look for a mathematics book — the very request that led to that tragic moment.
"But perhaps there is a blessing in disguise," she added, expressing concern that he might be bullied at school because of the incident.
Meanwhile, Nadia's lawyer, Mohd Faizi Che Abu, said they are considering civil action against the accused, with potential damages approaching RM1 million.
Earlier, Saktygaanapathy, a technician, was charged at the magistrate's court with the murder of Amirul, who was a warehouse worker and delivery rider, at 11.47am along Jalan Raya Barat on March 29.
No plea was recorded as murder cases fall under the jurisdiction of the High Court.
In a separate case, Saktygaanapathy pleaded guilty to self-administering benzodiazepine and tetrahydrocannabinol (THC) at 3.30pm on March 29 at the Klang Selatan narcotics office.
Amirul was killed instantly after being struck by the vehicle, allegedly travelling at high speed towards Klang town from Jalan Raya Barat.
The accused is believed to have overtaken several vehicles before veering into the opposite lane and colliding head-on with the motorcycle.
The impact flung Amirul onto a Perodua Myvi. He was pronounced dead at the scene.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: No notice filed for Azam Baki rally, say police
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Police have yet to receive any notification from organisers of the "Tangkap Azam Baki" rally, scheduled to be held at Dataran Merdeka on April 25.
Dang Wangi police chief Assistant Commissioner Sazalee Adam said that, as of today, no application or notification had been submitted to the authorities.
"Police advise the public to comply with the law and not to use assemblies as a platform for actions that could disrupt public order or threaten public safety," he said.
On Monday, the Tangkap Azam Baki Secretariat announced plans to hold the rally to demand a transparent investigation into the share ownership issue involving Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) chief commissioner Tan Sri Azam Baki, as well as allegations of corporate mafia influence within enforcement agencies.
The secretariat said both issues remained unresolved, raising concerns over the integrity of the MACC and the country's enforcement institutions.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Four-year-old boy undergoes 50 rounds of chemotherapy in leukaemia battle
Article:
BATU PAHAT: A mother has shared her ordeal after her four-year-old son underwent more than 50 rounds of chemotherapy following a leukaemia diagnosis in July last year.
Iffat Qaleef Mohamad Iqbal Akhim's condition first became apparent when he suffered frequent fevers before losing the ability to walk due to severe leg pain.
His parents, Nur Qamarina Noor Azmi, 26, and Mohamad Iqbal Akhim Mahat, 30, initially sought traditional treatment, but the relief was short-lived.
"His face turned pale and his stomach became swollen, with visible veins. We took him to the hospital, where blood tests returned abnormal results twice," Nur Qamarina said when met at her rented home in Taman Ampang Jaya here today.
Following a bone marrow biopsy at Sultanah Aminah Hospital in Johor Baru, Iffat Qaleef was diagnosed with leukaemia.
She said doctors classified his condition as standard risk, and he immediately began his first round of chemotherapy, administered every three hours over seven days.
The treatment journey has since been fraught with complications. During his second cycle, his liver was affected, leading to jaundice, while subsequent infections required a medical port to be fitted to his chest for medication.
Nur Qamarina said the financial strain had been overwhelming. Her husband was forced to resign from his job as a lorry driver to care for their son and now works as a handyman.
"His income is not enough to cover the cost of frequent trips to Johor Baru for treatment. At one point, he had to sell his motorcycle to pay for the expenses," she said.
Recent tests showed cancer cell levels at 0.003, with doctors aiming to reduce this to 0.001, indicating that traces of the disease remain.
In the search for a potential donor, the couple's other children — Nur Qalaisha Imani, 8, and Iman Qalis, 6 — were tested.
"Nur Qalaisha is a 100 per cent match. If the disease returns, the only option left is a bone marrow transplant from his sister.
"We are awaiting the latest test results. If cancer cells are still present, he will need to undergo the transplant at Canselor Tuanku Muhriz Hospital in Kuala Lumpur.
"The hospital has informed us that a transplant is not guaranteed to succeed. If it fails, the final option would be palliative care," she said.
Earlier, Batu Pahat Umno Welfare Bureau chairman Hamidi Mad Tap visited the family to assess their needs and coordinate assistance from the relevant authorities.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'My child blames herself': Court hears emotional account in Zara inquest
Article:
KOTA KINABALU: A mother told the Coroner's Court that her child became emotional, distressed and was often sobbing when recounting an incident involving the late Zara Qairina Mahathir.
The 41-year-old woman said her child, the 65th witness in the inquest, first informed her of the incident on July 16, 2025, while they were at Stadium Hoki Likas for a match.
Her child said Zara had jumped from the hostel building and asked her to pray for her.
However, she said her child did not provide further details at the time.
"Nearly three months later, my child finally told me in more detail what she knew about the incident," she said while reading her witness statement before coroner Amir Shah Amir Hassan.
According to her, the child said she had planned to meet a friend at Block A after prep time on July 15, 2025, but fell asleep.
She later woke up at about 1am and went to Block A. While returning to her room in Block B, she saw a female student walking along Level 3 of Block A.
The child went up and tapped the student on the back. When the student turned around, she realised it was Zara.
"My child told me Zara was crying and said she wanted to jump," the witness said, adding that her child asked, "Do you think you will go to heaven if you jump?"
She said her child then held Zara's hand and brought her back into her room before returning to her own dormitory.
The witness also told the court that her child was later questioned by a female police officer during a gathering at the school.
"My child told everything to the officer, but she raised her voice and asked why my child had gone to Block A at that time.
"My child began to cry. A male police officer then calmed her down, and she told him everything, but she does not know whether her statement was recorded," she said.
Following the incident, the mother said her child became withdrawn, appeared to blame herself and experienced severe trauma.
"When we asked about Zara, my child would only say, 'Mum, just recite Al-Fatihah for her.' I could see her emotions were unstable," she said.
She added that her child had noticed the day before the incident that Zara was not her usual cheerful self, responding only with a smile when approached.
"My child blames herself for not speaking to Zara, thinking that if she had done so, the incident might not have happened. At the time, she was busy with a school hockey competition and training," she said.
The witness said her child only shared the full account months later as she had been too traumatised to speak earlier.
"Every time Zara was mentioned, she would cry and could not respond. Zara's death had a deep impact on her as she knew her personally," she said.
The mother later contacted a teacher at SMKA Tun Datu Mustapha and informed her of the matter. Police subsequently recorded her statement.
Zara, 13, was found unconscious on the ground floor of her hostel and was pronounced dead on July 17, 2025, at Hospital Queen Elizabeth.
For the record, a new child witness testified in court yesterday and was said to have provided new information.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Woman killed after car skids off road and hits palm tree
Article:
KOTA TINGGI: A 36-year-old woman was killed after her car lost control and veered off Km2 of Jalan Lok Heng-Mawai, crashing into a palm tree earlier today.
District police chief Superintendent Yusof Othman said the driver sustained severe internal injuries and was pronounced dead at the scene.
Her body has been sent to Kota Tinggi Hospital's forensic unit for further examination.
The case is being investigated under Section 41(1) of the Road Transport Act 1987, which carries a jail term of five to 10 years, fines of RM20,000 to RM50,000, and a minimum five-year driving disqualification upon conviction.
Yusof urged all motorists to remain vigilant and adhere to traffic regulations to ensure road safety for everyone.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Convenience store worker pleads guilty to RM32,000 theft to settle Ah Long debts
Article:
BATU PAHAT: A convenience store worker was charged at the Sessions Court here today with seven counts of criminal breach of trust (CBT), involving more than RM32,000 allegedly used to settle debts with illegal moneylenders.
The 23-year-old Nurfatin Najwa Ahmad Bzli pleaded guilty to all charges before Judge Osman Affendi Mohd Shalleh.
She is accused of misappropriating RM32,101.70 from daily sales proceeds belonging to the store.
The offences were committed at a convenience store in Taman Soga between March 21 and 27.
All charges were framed under Section 408 of the Penal Code, which carries a maximum jail term of 14 years, a fine, and whipping upon conviction. However, Section 289 of the Criminal Procedure Code exempts women from the sentence of whipping.
The alleged misconduct came to light after store management noticed irregularities in cash flow and conducted an audit.
Nurfatin had been assigned to perform stock counts and man the payment counter on March 19, covering for a colleague on maternity leave.
An inventory audit later revealed discrepancies between stock and daily revenue.
Nurfatin reportedly told the store manager she had taken the proceeds to repay Ah Longs, whose enforcers were allegedly intimidating her.
Deputy public prosecutor Sharifah Natasha Syed Ahmad appeared for the prosecution, while Nurfatin was unrepresented.
Following her guilty plea, the prosecution did not seek bail and requested a date for the mention of facts and sentencing.
The court denied bail and set the next mention for Friday.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Cloud seeding to boost dam levels as heatwave grips parts of Malaysia
Article:
PUTRAJAYA: The National Disaster Management Agency (Nadma) will carry out four rounds of cloud seeding operations from April 6 to 9 in Perak, Kedah, Perlis and Johor to boost water levels in dams affected by prolonged hot weather.
Deputy Prime Minister Datuk Seri Dr Ahmad Zahid Hamidi said the operations were planned following the ongoing hot weather linked to the monsoon transition, which began on March 29 and is expected to continue until May.
"Although this period brings thunderstorms and heavy rain in parts of Peninsular Malaysia, as well as Sabah and Sarawak, several areas remain at risk of Level One and Level Two heatwaves, with temperatures reaching between 35 and 40 degrees Celsius.
"These conditions have not significantly increased dam water levels, affecting water resources, the agricultural sector and daily life, particularly for vulnerable communities in rural and interior areas," said the chairman of the Central Disaster Management Committee in a statement.
The operations will be carried out with the Royal Malaysian Air Force providing C-130 Hercules aircraft, while the Malaysian Meteorological Department (MetMalaysia) will coordinate technical and scientific aspects.
Cloud seeding will focus on 11 dams — Timah Tasoh in Perlis; Ahning, Pedu and Muda in Kedah; Temenggor and Bukit Merah in Perak; and Lebam, Upper Layang, Lower Layang, Gunung Pulai and Machap in Johor.
"This measure is a proactive effort to restore water levels affected by prolonged hot weather," Zahid said.
He added that state disaster committees have been instructed to identify medium-term measures, including water catchment strategies during the rainy season for use in dry periods.
As part of preparedness efforts, the State Disaster Command Centre (SDCC) will be activated and strengthened to ensure decisions are based on technical data and analysis.
All relevant agencies have been directed to channel information to the SDCC, including data on dam levels, raw water supply, soil moisture, landslide risks and flood-prone areas.
Zahid said state authorities must act early by identifying the needs of affected communities and sectors, including providing emergency water supplies via tanker lorries where disruptions occur.
He also called for a comprehensive assessment by Nadma to develop long-term mitigation plans based on data from state agencies.
At the same time, he urged all government bodies to intensify efforts to protect sectors likely to be affected, including food supply, agriculture, public health and education.
The public has also been advised to use water prudently and follow official instructions.
"In any situation, the government's principle is clear — the safety and well-being of the people remain the highest priority, and no one will be left to face this alone," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: PM Anwar's phone-call diplomacy wins safe Hormuz access
Article:
Let's give credit when it's due.
The decision of Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim to engage in relentless phone-call diplomacy - and many foreign visits - is now yielding tangible, measurable results.
Few outcomes are as strategically significant as Iran allowing Malaysian vessels to pass through the Straits of Hormuz.
According to news reports, about 50 per cent of Malaysia's total oil passes through the narrow strip.
The Prime Minister had called up Iranian president Masoud Pezeskian, while Foreign Minister Datuk Seri Mohamad Hasan also spoke to his counterpart, Abbas Aragchi, to allow seven ships to continue their journey home, of which four are carrying crude oil.
Iran's Ambassador to Malaysia, Valiollah Mohammadi Nasrabadi, has stated that the vessels would be allowed to pass through the strait safely - and free of charge.
As of late March 2026, reports indicate that Iran has begun demanding "transit fees" or "tolls" from ships navigating the Strait of Hormuz, with some reports suggesting charges of up to US$2 million per vessel.
It is not just a symbolic win but a hard geopolitical achievement. Malaysia has always strongly defended its neutrality.
Malaysia is a friend to everyone, but we are not afraid to speak up when something isn't right, including the attacks on Iran and the Palestinians in Gaza.
The passage through the Straits of Hormuz is crucial, as while Malaysia produces its own oil, it imports significant amounts of crude from the Persian Gulf, which is roughly 69 per cent of its crude oil imports, to keep domestic refineries running.
Malaysia exports its own lighter, higher-priced crude while importing heavier, cheaper oil from the Middle East that passes through the Strait of Hormuz.
The closure of the Strait of Hormuz has created significant economic pressure, forcing the government to increase fuel subsidies to control domestic prices.
At a time when the Strait of Hormuz - through which roughly a fifth of global oil supply flows - has effectively become a conflict zone, Iran has been restricting passage and asserting tight control over shipping.
Yet, Malaysia has secured a special carve-out, with its tankers and crews allowed safe passage following direct engagement with Tehran and other regional powers.
Why does Malaysia's foreign policy work?
First, Anwar has positioned Malaysia as an honest broker, not a partisan actor. Unlike major powers entangled militarily or politically, Malaysia has consistently called for de-escalation and dialogue, rejecting military involvement while maintaining moral clarity on the conflict.  This neutrality gives Kuala Lumpur credibility in Tehran.
Second, Anwar's personalised diplomacy matters. In an era of fractured multilateralism, direct leader-to-leader communication - phone calls with Iranian, Turkish and Egyptian counterparts - cuts through bureaucracy and builds trust.
These are not mere formalities; they signal respect, recognition, and seriousness to a country like Iran that feels strategically isolated and "repeatedly deceived" by global powers.
Third, Malaysia brings economic relevance without a strategic threat. Iran understands that Malaysia is a major energy consumer and trading partner, not a military adversary.
Allowing Malaysian ships through does not weaken Iran's geopolitical posture, but strengthens its ties with a key Southeast Asian nation.
We may be a small country, but middle powers like Malaysia can still exercise influence, not through force, but through credibility, consistency and communication.
Let's not forget that when our 23 Malaysian volunteers from the Global Sumud Flotilla were detained by Israel while on a Gaza aid mission, they were freed following intense diplomatic efforts led by Anwar last October.
It was the result of many phone calls to numerous leaders, including the influential and powerful President Recep Tayyip Erdogan of Turkey.
During peacetime, it is easy to overlook the quiet effectiveness of diplomacy conducted over the phone.
Yet, the release of Malaysian tankers and the reopening of a critical supply route show that such diplomacy is not naive - it is pragmatic and consequential.
Anwar's approach demonstrates that even in a highly polarised conflict, access is negotiated, not assumed.
And Malaysia, by choosing engagement over alignment, has secured something many larger nations have not - trust. That's the real strategic capital in international diplomacy.
– BERNAMA
Datuk Seri Wong Chun Wai is the chairman of Bernama and a National Journalism Laureate.
The views expressed in this article are the author's own and do not necessarily reflect those of the New Straits Times
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Customs crackdown: RM2.4mil in smuggled rice, meat, and e-bikes seized
Article:
IPOH: The Perak Customs Department has cracked down on a string of smuggling attempts involving rice, frozen meat, tiles, and electric bicycles, with total duties and taxes estimated at RM2.4 million.
Its director, Mohd Nadzri Ariffin, said it was a result of coordinated operations by its units in Teluk Intan, Ipoh, and Pengkalan Hulu.
"This reflects our continuous efforts to curb smuggling and false declarations, which not only result in revenue losses but also threaten national security and public wellbeing," he said at a press conference.
In one operation, he said officers from the Teluk Intan branch inspected a container at Port Klang on Jan 14 and uncovered 801 cartons falsely declared as tiles.
"The seizure is valued at RM406,500, with duties and taxes totalling RM557,790.
"A 35-year-old man was arrested for failing to obtain the required permit from the Construction Industry Development Board (CIDB)," he told reporters at the department headquarters here, today.
He said another operation on Jan 27 saw 765 sacks of rice, weighing 22.9 tonnes, seized at Port Klang after being falsely declared as fried gram.
"The rice is worth RM201,660, with duties estimated at RM105,656.40. Investigations revealed the importer had also failed to obtain a Bernas permit," he said.
In Perak, he said officers intercepted a lorry along Jalan Lenggong–Gerik carrying undeclared rice.
"The driver and his attendant, aged 28 and 30, were detained. The goods are valued at RM89,376, with duties of RM35,750, and lacked the required permit," he said.
Mohd Nadzri also highlighted a Feb 12 operation at Port Klang where two containers were found to contain 49,343kg of frozen meat falsely declared as frozen dumplings.
"The meat is valued at RM614,832.26, with duties of RM30,741.61, and a local man believed to be a forwarding agent representative was detained," he said.
On Feb 6, he said a total of 418 electric bicycles were seized at Westport, Port Klang, after being falsely declared.
"Valued at RM385,244.27, the items were imported from China but falsely declared as Malaysian products through fraudulent certificates of origin. A company manager was detained," he said.
Members of the public with information on suspected smuggling activities can channel it via the Customs toll-free hotline at 1-800-88-8855 or the nearest Customs office.
The identity of informants will be kept confidential.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Johor KPDN ramps up petrol station checks as RON95 ban begins
Article:
JOHOR BARU: The Johor Domestic Trade and Cost of Living Ministry (KPDN) has stepped up monitoring at all 639 petrol stations in the state following the enforcement of the RON95 petrol sales ban on foreign-registered vehicles, effective today.
State director Lilis Saslinda Pornomo said heightened surveillance would focus on 31 petrol stations near the Singapore border to prevent fuel leakages.
"We are enhancing existing monitoring, including deploying motorcycle patrol teams and increasing the presence of enforcement officers. Monitoring is being conducted at all petrol stations, particularly in border areas and other high-risk locations, with over 180 enforcement officers stationed across Johor," she said when contacted today.
Lilis Saslinda said that petrol station operators in the state had been notified in advance, with information shared through major oil companies and operators.
"We believe operators are well-informed, as the directive has been publicised and communicated widely. Oil companies have even held engagement sessions with their dealers and station operators to ensure compliance," she said.
Follow-up inspections will also be carried out to ensure all petrol stations display notices regarding the ban.
"We will continue ground inspections to confirm that operators display the notices and that station staff are aware of the implementation, especially at locations frequented by foreign vehicles," she added.
Checks by the New Straits Times at city petrol stations found that drivers of foreign-registered vehicles, particularly Singaporeans, were complying with the new regulation.
A Singaporean, identified only as Sajali Ali, 43, said he was aware of the enforcement as he frequently visits Johor Baru.
"Besides shopping, I came to Johor Baru to celebrate Hari Raya with my mother's side of the family. They informed me about the enforcement. For me, it is not an issue because I have always purchased RON97 anyway," he said.
Earlier, Domestic Trade and Cost of Living Minister Armizan Mohd Ali said the government would
expand the enforcement
of the RON95 petrol ban on foreign-registered vehicles from today.
He said that the move strengthened the legal framework, which previously only penalised petrol station operators.
Under the expanded enforcement, drivers or owners of foreign-registered vehicles found in breach of the regulation will also face penalties, ensuring RON95 subsidies benefit only locals and preventing fuel leakages, particularly in border states such as Johor.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Dispel myth of 'healthy' alcohol consumption
Article:
LETTERS:
We often reassure ourselves that alcohol is only a marginal issue in Malaysia. After all, just 10.4 per cent of Malaysians report imbibing alcohol, according to the National Health and Morbidity Survey (NHMS) 2023.
However, that figure conceals a far more troubling reality. Among those who drink, nearly one in two engage in binge drinking — consuming six or more drinks in a single sitting. This is a high-risk behaviour.
In other words, Malaysia has a dangerous alcohol consumption problem. Yet we continue to cling to a comforting myth: that alcohol, in moderation, is safe. It is not.
For years, the public has been led to believe that small amounts of alcohol — a glass of wine, an occasional drink — may even offer health benefits. This narrative is not only outdated, but it is also misleading.
Alcohol is classified as a Group 1 carcinogen by the International Agency for Research on Cancer, placing it in the same category as tobacco, asbestos and radiation. It is known to cause at least seven types of cancer, including breast and colorectal cancer. It is an established scientific fact.
Evidence published in The Lancet Public Health makes this clear — no level of alcohol consumption is completely safe. So, why does the myth persist?
Alcohol remains deeply normalised — embedded in social settings, promoted through marketing, and subjected to far less scrutiny than tobacco. Yet its consequences are no less serious: road fatalities, preventable diseases and families torn apart.
The Health Ministry has consistently highlighted the risks of alcohol. But awareness alone is not enough. If we are serious about prevention, it must move beyond education and address the structural drivers of harm.
We already know what works: increase taxes to reduce affordability, restrict advertising — especially to young people — and limit its availability.
These are not radical proposals. They are evidence-based measures that have proven effective in tobacco control and are increasingly recognised as essential in tackling other major public health challenges.
Thus, the evidence is clear, and the solutions are known.
DR MUHAMMAD IKHWAN MUD SHUKRI
Public health physician,
Bagan Datuk, Perak
The views expressed in this article are the author's own and do not necessarily reflect those of the New Straits Times
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Where is Pamela Ling? Family seeks answers nearly a year after her disappearance
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The family of Pamela Ling, who disappeared nearly a year ago, has called for urgent action and accountability from authorities, as investigations into her disappearance remain unresolved.
The family, through their lawyer, Sangeet Kaur Deo, said the investigation seemed to lack direction and seriousness from the outset, leaving her family without answers.
She added that repeated interrogations, prolonged travel restrictions, and alleged heavy-handed conduct by the Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) preceded Ling's disappearance.
"Almost one year on, and after months of silence from the police, the family was recently met with a sudden request for basic information, including what steps were taken upon the discovery of her disappearance.
"These are questions that had already been posed at the earliest stages," Sangeet said in a statement.
"One year on, the police have come back with nothing. No clear account of what investigations have been carried out or whether the concerns raised by Ling prior to her disappearance were examined has been provided at all."
Ling, a mother and businesswoman, had been involved in civil disputes over share ownership and divorce proceedings when she was summoned by MACC in January 2025.
She was arrested, remanded, and released on bail with reporting conditions, including a travel ban, which she fully complied with.
Ling was reported
missing
on April 9, 2025, shortly before her planned meeting at the MACC headquarters in Putrajaya.
She was reportedly taken from an e-hailing vehicle. Police later said five vehicles using
false registration plates
were involved in the suspected abduction.
Prior to her disappearance, she had filed a judicial review against MACC, alleging abuse of power, unlawful detention, and violations of her constitutional rights.
Sangeet said the circumstances surrounding her disappearance raise serious questions about potential abuse of power by public institutions.
"The averments in her final sworn affidavit stand as Ling's last act of defiance, a refusal to back down in the face of what she described as sustained pressure and abuse of power," she said.
Sangeet added that the government must act to ensure transparency and accountability, not only for Ling's family but also to restore public confidence in state institutions.
Last June, it was reported that police
recorded statements
from 49 people, including Ling's husband and children.
A composite sketch of the suspect has not led to any conclusive matches.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: [UPDATED] MACC offers to fly in James Chai as RM1.1bil Arm probe intensifies
Article:
PUTRAJAYA: The Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) has offered to cover travel costs for James Chai to return to Malaysia to assist in investigations into alleged misconduct linked to a RM1.1 billion government deal with Arm Holdings signed in March last year.
The commission said statements have been recorded from 17 witnesses so far, including officers from the Economy Ministry and related agencies such as the Malaysian Investment Development Authority (Mida).
In a statement today, MACC said investigators have also obtained key documents, including agreements and supporting materials, and are expected to record statements from a further eight individuals, including those currently overseas.
The MACC said Chai, also known as Chai Jin Shern, is required to assist in the investigation.
"A Notice to Locate him was issued on March 4.
"The commission later received a letter from his solicitors dated March 9, stating that he was unable to attend for questioning due to travel costs and work commitments," it said, adding that they subsequently replied on March 13, granting him an extension to appear on March 31.
It said however, Chai did not indicate any intention to comply.
"In light of this, the MACC has issued a letter today requiring him to attend within two weeks.
"The commission has also expressed its willingness to bear the cost of his return flight to Malaysia," it said.
The commission warned that failure to comply after being granted two extensions may result in it seeking assistance through international channels, including Interpol and foreign enforcement agencies, to locate him and ensure his attendance.
"If James Chai maintains that he has committed no wrongdoing, the appropriate course of action is to
present himself to the MACC
to provide a statement, rather than issuing explanations in the public domain without appearing before the authorities," it said.
Early last month,
the MACC called on the political analyst to return to Malaysia
and present himself to investigators, saying he has yet to respond despite multiple attempts to contact him.
The MACC did not disclose the nature of the investigation but said cooperation with law enforcement was a basic obligation of any citizen when required to assist.
It was previously reported that Chai questioned the MACC for calling him a "wanted person", insisting that he is "a nobody."
This follows the MACC's earlier statement seeking Chai, who previously served as a special functions officer to Datuk Seri Rafizi Ramli, who is the former economy minister, to assist in an investigation involving a RM1.1 billion project between the UK based firm and Putrajaya.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Johor MB courts Indian professionals as state sharpens economic push
Article:
JOHOR BARU: Johor Menteri Besar Datuk Onn Hafiz Ghazi has stepped up engagement with the Indian professional community, positioning them as a strategic partner in the state's economic ambitions.
The move comes amid rising global uncertainty and mounting cost-of-living pressures.
Onn Hafiz said the state government was acting in line with the call of Tunku Mahkota Ismail, Regent of Johor, who has consistently stressed the importance of safeguarding the people's welfare.
He warned that geopolitical tensions, including ongoing conflicts in the Middle East, were adding to global volatility, compelling Johor to adopt early and structured measures to cushion the impact on its rakyat.
"In facing rising cost-of-living pressures, the state government is refining targeted approaches to ease the burden on the people. Several comprehensive and high-impact initiatives will be announced soon," he said.
The menteri besar said the measures would be designed to preserve public welfare to ensure balanced and sustainable economic growth.
On Sunday (March 29), Onn Hafiz held an engagement session with Indian professionals in Johor, spanning sectors such as business, education, engineering, law and industry.
Also present were MIC youth chief K. Raven Kumar.
Raven, who is also the state Tourism, Environment, Heritage and Culture Committee chairman, described the engagement as constructive and open as it provided a vital platform for Indian professionals to share views, exchange ideas and propose measures to strengthen socio-economic development, education and inclusive career opportunities in the state.
Onn Hafiz said the session was a critical platform that reflected the strength and untapped potential of the Indian community in the states.
"The community's expertise and leadership were vital to Johor's development trajectory.
"The development of Johor is not driven by policy alone, but strengthened by the experience, knowledge and leadership of Bangsa Johor, regardless of race or religion," he said.
The session also allowed the menteri besar to outline the state's economic direction, with particular emphasis on the Johor-Singapore Special Economic Zone (JS-SEZ), which he described as a key catalyst for growth.
Onn Hafiz said the JS-SEZ would open the door to quality investments, create more high-income job opportunities and elevate Johor's competitiveness at both regional and global levels.
He added that feedback from the Indian professional community, including ideas, proposals and aspirations, would be factored into policy planning to ensure more inclusive and effective outcomes.
Previously, MIC said it will
remain with Barisan Nasional (BN)
following a Central Working Committee meeting in Kuala Lumpur on March 25.
Citing leadership changes within Perikatan Nasional (PN), MIC deputy president Datuk Seri M. Saravanan said the decision came amid renewed political noise after PN secretary-general Datuk Seri Takiyuddin Hassan claimed the party had previously
applied to join the coalition
.
Takiyuddin cited a letter addressed to PN chairman Tan Sri Muhyiddin Yassin as evidence.
However, MIC has maintained that the correspondence was merely a request for clarification on PN's structure and terms, and not an application to join.
Saravanan said the party is likely to remain with BN at least until the next general election.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: #NSTviral: Tears of pride: Fried banana seller weeps as son scores 11A+ in SPM [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A father was overcome with emotion as his son received his SPM results after scoring 11A+.
In a 52-second video shared on TikTok by user @tvpss.smatkem and reported by SiakapKeli, 2025 SPM candidate Fakhrul Iman is seen celebrating the achievement with his family.
The clip shows his father, Zainudin Abdul Latif, holding his son's face and crying tears of joy.
@tvpss.smatkem
Tahniah Fakhrul Iman atas kecemerlangan
#spm2025
#batch08
#smatkem
#straightaplus
original sound  - TV PSS SMA TELOK KEMANG
Zainudin, a fried banana seller in Telok Kemang, appeared visibly moved by his son's success.
Fakhrul attributed his achievement to respect for teachers, saying discipline and good manners helped him better understand his lessons.
"To me, teachers are not just educators — they are like parents at school and play a major role in shaping students' success," he said.
He plans to further his studies abroad, particularly in the Middle East, in the field of religion.
"If I achieve this, God willing, I aspire to become a lecturer," he said in an interview.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: MyNews poised for earnings rebound on aggressive expansion, product push: CIMB Securities
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: MyNews Holdings Bhd is expected to see a rebound in earnings in the second half of financial year 2026 (FY26), driven by an ambitious store rollout and enhanced product offerings, according to CIMB Securities Sdn Bhd.
The firm said the company's performance may be seasonally softer in the second quarter ending April 30, 2026, due to the Ramadan fasting period but expects earnings momentum to improve in the following quarters.
It noted that the group aims to open around 100 new stores in FY26, an 8.6 per cent increase from the end of FY25, with a particular focus on ready-to-eat food products to drive customer traffic and basket size.
"Alongside cost optimisation initiatives and plans to increase automation usage in its operations, a larger store base should improve economies of scale and utilisation at MyNews's food processing centre," CIMB Securities said in a note.
It said that MyNews's first-quarter core net profit (CNP) came in at RM4.3 million, missing expectations as ongoing store expansion plans led to higher-than-expected operating expenses.
The firm trimmed its FY26–28 earnings per share forecasts by 1.3–7.2 per cent to reflect higher operating expenses.
However, MyNews's CNP is projected to grow 20.9 per cent year-on-year in FY26, supported by new store openings, improved operating leverage and a more profitable sales mix.
CIMB Securities maintained a "Buy" rating on MyNews, revising the target price to 75 sen from 79 sen, based on a 21 times 2027 price-to-earnings (P/E) multiple.
The lower TP accounts for higher operating costs and a smaller store footprint relative to peers like 7-Eleven.
The firm said that at 17 times 2026 P/E, MyNews remains attractively valued, supported by a strong earnings trajectory with a projected three-year core net profit compound annual growth rate of 16.2 per cent from FY25–28 and its established brand presence in Malaysia's convenience store sector.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Police seek help to locate woman missing in Penang
Article:
GEORGE TOWN: Police are seeking public assistance to locate a 39-year-old woman who went missing after leaving her home last week.
Seberang Prai Selatan police chief Superintendent Jay January Siowou identified the woman as Nur Ain Ariffen, who was reported missing on March 26.
He said efforts to locate her are underway.
"Members of the public who have any information or who may have seen the woman are urged to come forward to assist in the investigation," he said in a statement.
Jay said information can be conveyed to investigating officer Sergeant Major T. Kangeswari at 010-375 0353 or at any police station.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia's financial system resilient despite external headwinds: RHB Research
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia's financial system remains resilient despite rising external risks, with businesses and households entering a potentially more challenging environment from a position of strength, according to RHB Research.
This follows the briefing by Bank Negara Malaysia in conjunction with the release of its Financial Stability Review for the second half of 2025 (2H25) on Tuesday.
RHB Research noted that while the ongoing Middle East conflict is expected to pose headwinds to growth and cost pressures, larger corporates are better positioned to weather the impact due to stronger financial buffers and bargaining power.
Smaller businesses, however, could be more vulnerable, although existing government support measures are expected to provide some relief.
The firm added that banks continue to maintain adequate provision buffers, which should help cushion potential stress during this period.
It has maintained an "Overweight" call on the banking sector, naming Public Bank Bhd, CIMB Group Holdings Bhd, and Malayan Banking Bhd (Maybank) as its top picks.
Meanwhile, the central bank's assessment showed that business activity in the second half of 2025 remained supported by resilient domestic demand.
While cost pressures persisted, they were partly offset by lower imported input costs and a firmer ringgit.
Key financial indicators improved, with the share of firms at risk declining to 23.7 per cent from 25.9 per cent in June 2025.
The proportion of impaired business loans and those with increased credit risk also eased to 2.8 per cent and 10.7 per cent, respectively.
Looking ahead, Bank Negara expects the external environment to remain challenging, particularly if geopolitical tensions persist, which could lead to higher costs and slower growth.
On the household front, the central bank said conditions remain stable, supported by a healthy labour market.
Household debt-to-gross domestic product (GDP) stood at 84.8 per cent, while the median debt service ratio was 33 per cent.
The gross impaired loan ratio remained low at one per cent.
Buy Now Pay Later usage continued to expand, with about 7.5 million active users, although overdue debt levels were stable at 3.2 per cent.
Bank Negara noted that signs of financial stress among households remain limited and isolated, with no broad-based deterioration observed.
The central bank also projects Malaysia's economy to grow between four and five per cent in 2026, driven mainly by domestic demand, while investment activity is expected to remain supportive, albeit at a more moderate pace.
Headline inflation is forecast at between 1.5 and 2.5 per cent.
However, the central bank cautioned that risks from the Middle East conflict could materialise through higher import and production costs, weaker export growth, and more volatile capital flows.
These risks are expected to be partly mitigated by Malaysia's position as a net energy exporter and the presence of targeted subsidies.
Bank Negara also said its baseline projections assume the conflict will be resolved within one to two months, although it has also considered a more prolonged scenario of up to six months.
At present, feedback from industry engagements suggests businesses are not delaying or postponing investment plans.
On potential policy responses, the central bank said it is still early to assess the need for additional assistance packages, noting that existing measures, including special lower-rate financing facilities, remain available.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Dang Wangi police on alert ahead of Azam Baki rally
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Dang Wangi police are closely monitoring plans for a rally by the group "Sekretariat Tangkap Azam Baki" at Dataran Merdeka on April 25, even as no organisers have formally notified the authorities of the proposed gathering.
Dang Wangi police chief Assistant Commissioner Sazalee Adam said checks so far showed that no organisers had formally notified the authorities of the gathering.
"As of now, no application or notification has been submitted to the police regarding the proposed assembly," he said when contacted.
Sazalee urged the public to comply with existing laws and regulations, stressing that any assembly must not jeopardise public order or safety.
"The public is reminded to abide by the law and not use any gathering as a platform for actions that could disrupt peace or threaten public security," he said.
The group had earlier announced plans for the rally to demand a transparent investigation into allegations involving Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) chief commissioner Tan Sri Azam Baki, including questions over his share ownership and claims of a so-called "corporate mafia" within enforcement agencies.
Sekretariat Tangkap Azam Baki said both issues remained unresolved, raising concerns about the integrity of the MACC and national enforcement institutions.
Bersih deputy chairman Kelvin Lee said the decision to proceed with the rally stemmed from what he described as a lack of clear explanation and concrete action from the government.
"The secretariat has decided to take to the streets once again to demand a more comprehensive and transparent investigation into Azam's share ownership issue and the alleged corporate mafia, which has affected the reputation and integrity of the MACC.
"To date, there has been no clear, transparent, or conclusive explanation from Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim and the Madani government," he said at a press conference in Petaling Jaya on March 30.
Lee also criticised the government-appointed committee set up to look into the matter, describing it as a delay tactic.
"Despite the committee being established about a month ago, there has been no announcement or update so far," he said, adding that ongoing revelations linked to the alleged corporate mafia within enforcement agencies had further fuelled public concern over institutional integrity.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Six-vehicle pile-up in Kampar not caused by drink-driver
Article:
KAMPAR: Police have dismissed claims that a six-vehicle pile-up near a school in Malim Nawar yesterday was caused by a drink-driver.
The 6.42pm crash left five people injured, including two siblings aged five and seven who sustained minor head injuries.
District police chief Superintendent Mohamad Nazri Daud said initial investigations found no signs of intoxication in the driver, believed to have triggered the collision.
"The driver showed no signs of being under the influence of alcohol, and there was no smell of alcohol on his breath," he said.
He added that urine screening and blood alcohol tests are being conducted.
Nazri said the accident involved five cars and a motorcycle. A Proton Satria driven by a 59-year-old man is believed to have veered into the opposite lane before colliding head-on with a Perodua Viva.
"The impact caused the Viva, driven by a 31-year-old woman with two children, to overturn.
"The Proton Satria also struck a Yamaha Y15ZR motorcycle ridden by a 19-year-old woman, who was thrown onto the road. A Perodua Bezza approaching from Kuala Dipang was unable to avoid hitting the fallen motorcyclist despite braking," he said.
The chain reaction continued when the overturned Viva hit a parked Proton Saga, which was pushed back into a Perodua Axia parked behind it.
Nazri said the drivers of the Viva and Satria, along with the motorcyclist, were injured. The drivers of the other vehicles were unhurt.
The case is being investigated under Section 43(1) of the Road Transport Act for careless and inconsiderate driving.
A Facebook post had earlier alleged the crash occurred during the afternoon school pick-up and was caused by a drunk driver, claiming alcohol cans were found in the Satria.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Murder trial of Myanmar nationals postponed over language barrier
Article:
GEORGE TOWN: Proceedings against two Myanmar nationals charged with murdering a fellow countryman last month were postponed at the magistrate's court here today after it emerged that both men do not understand Malay.
The accused, Myat Thu Khaing and Sein Win Aung, were unable to have the charge read to them during the hearing due to the language barrier.
Deputy public prosecutor Siti Marissa Safina Saiful Adli requested the court to set a date for the appointment of a Myanmar-language interpreter to ensure the charge could be properly communicated.
Magistrate Tunku Intan Nadiah Tunku Mohammed Nazarin fixed April 23 for the interpreter to be appointed.
The case stems from an incident earlier last month in which a man was found dead in an open area near Gat Lebuh Macallum.
On March 9, police said that the
victim was discovered lying motionless
with visible head injuries, believed to have been caused by a blunt object.
Northeast district police chief Assistant Commissioner Abdul Rozak Muhammad said initial checks revealed the victim had no identification documents and was believed to be a foreign national in his 20s.
A post-mortem conducted by the Forensic Medical Department at Penang Hospital confirmed that the cause of death was blunt force trauma to the head.
The case is being investigated under Section 302 of the Penal Code for murder.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Sabah sets up tourism crisis unit amid global uncertainty
Article:
KOTA KINABALU: The Sabah Tourism Board (STB) will establish a dedicated crisis response unit to safeguard the state's tourism industry amid growing global uncertainties.
The move comes against the backdrop of ongoing geopolitical tensions in the Middle East and rising operational costs, which could affect travel demand and industry stability.
STB chief executive officer Julinus Jimit will take on an additional role as chief strategy officer as part of an organisational restructuring, overseeing planning, coordination and response measures across the sector.
The decision was made during an STB board meeting chaired by Assistant Minister to the Chief Minister and STB chairman Datuk Joniston Bangkuai yesterday, following concerns over external factors that could impact travel demand, airline operations and market stability.
The Tourism Crisis Response Unit will report directly to the board of directors to ensure close oversight and timely updates on developments and strategic decisions.
Joniston said that while Sabah's tourism industry is on a recovery path, the global operating environment is becoming increasingly volatile.
He warned that geopolitical tensions, particularly in the Middle East, could influence both international and domestic travel patterns, fuel prices and airline route planning — with potential knock-on effects on Sabah's connectivity and visitor arrivals.
"As a destination with strong international linkages, we must stay alert and respond proactively. These developments require close monitoring and continuous engagement with airlines and industry players," he said.
The unit will focus on mitigating external shocks, including fluctuations in fuel prices, while ensuring Sabah remains competitive, accessible and attractive to both international and domestic markets.
It will also facilitate regular engagement with key stakeholders — including hoteliers, airlines and tour operators — to strengthen coordination, align messaging and enable quicker responses to emerging challenges.
STB will also intensify its focus on the domestic market as a stabilising force amid global uncertainties.
Separately, the board proposed forming a joint STB–Kota Kinabalu City Hall (DBKK) Tourism Development and Marketing Committee to improve coordination in developing and promoting tourism products in the state capital.
The committee will identify new attractions, strengthen existing offerings and enhance marketing efforts for Kota Kinabalu as a key gateway to Sabah.
"We cannot afford to work in silos. Our strategies must be coordinated and aligned with industry players, airlines and communities. With Visit Sabah 2026 ahead, we need to move forward in a focused and united manner," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Family calls off wedding, suffers RM150,000 loss to keep daughter safe
Article:
KOTA BARU: The wedding of cosmetic entrepreneur Fara Sharina Zamari's daughter, Putri Fasha Felisa Mohd Izhan, 19, was called off after the teenager was assaulted by her then-fiancé.
Fara Sharina, 37, said the ceremony, originally scheduled for yesterday, was cancelled upon learning of the incident, which left her daughter injured.
"I would rather my daughter's wedding be cancelled than have something worse happen to her after she is married," she told reporters.
Preparations for the special day were nearly complete, with invitations set to be sent out at the start of Ramadan. The engagement, which had lasted three months, was immediately terminated following the assault.
"All the wedding preparations were almost ready, including my daughter's choice of an English-style theme at Padang Polo, Kemumin. Once the assault came to light, the engagement was called off and the ring returned. There was no objection from the groom's side," she said.
The wedding had been scheduled for March 31, coinciding with Putri Fasha Felisa's birthday on Aug 31, with a dowry agreed at RM31,000.
[[nid:1407984]]
Fara Sharina added that the family incurred a financial loss of around RM150,000 on event expenses, including catering, the pelamin (wedding dais), and outfits for relatives.
"Despite the financial setback, my daughter's wellbeing is our priority. The trauma she suffered far outweighs any monetary loss, and our focus is on her emotional recovery," she said.
"The incident was heartbreaking, as our only daughter was treated this way, but we are grateful it happened before marriage. It has left a lasting lesson, and she now intends to focus on her business rather than marriage."
Previously, it was reported that the
ex-fiancé had allegedly punched,
choked, kicked and dragged the victim, causing bruises and swelling across her body.
On March 18, the 20-year-old was fined RM1,500 after being found guilty of intentionally causing injury.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: [UPDATED] Annas Sulaiman appointed as new Wangsa Maju police chief
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Crime buster Assistant Commissioner Annas Sulaiman has been appointed the new Wangsa Maju police chief.
He succeeds Senior Assistant Commissioner Mohamad Lazim Ismail, who has been promoted to Bukit Aman as the Commercial Crime Investigation Department (CCID) deputy director (strategy).
The handing-over-of-duties ceremony was witnessed by Kuala Lumpur police chief Datuk Fadil Marsus this morning at the Kuala Lumpur police headquarters.
Annas' previous roles include chief of the Ampang Jaya Criminal Investigation Division, deputy police chief of Cheras, and Director of Integrated Operations at the National Financial Crime Prevention Centre (NFCC).
In his address, Fadil highlighted the importance of strong leadership, integrity and adaptability in tackling the growing complexities of policing, particularly in a metropolitan area such as Kuala Lumpur.
"The successes of the Wangsa Maju district reflect not only operational effectiveness, but also the professionalism and commitment of its personnel," he said, praising Mohamad Lazim's tenure as a model of dedication and steady leadership.
Fadil noted that Lazim's move to a strategic role within the CCID demonstrated confidence in his ability to address evolving crime trends, including cybercrime.
"Leadership transitions strengthen the organisation, ensuring expertise is placed where it is most needed," he said.
He urged all personnel to maintain integrity, discipline and teamwork in delivering effective policing services to the public.
In his farewell speech, Lazim expressed gratitude to police leadership, colleagues and the community for their support.
He acknowledged the challenges of policing, particularly in maintaining public safety and order, but stressed that these were met through collective effort.
"The trust placed in the police must be continuously earned through professionalism, dedication and responsiveness to the needs of the community," he said, noting the importance of organisational transformation and adapting to future demands.
Reflecting on his tenure in Wangsa Maju, he thanked officers and personnel for their contributions and apologised for any shortcomings.
"I hope the team will continue to strengthen its efforts and uphold the values of the police force," he added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Police appeal for leads on two individuals missing since March 25 and 26 in Nibong Tebal
Article:
NIBONG TEBAL, April 1 — Police are actively trying to locate two individuals who have been reported missing after leaving their respective homes on March 25 and 26, and have not returned since.
Seberang Perai Selatan police chief Supt Jay January Siowou said that the first individual is a 44-year-old man, Lim Hock Seng, who has been missing since March 25.
“The other is a woman, Nur Ain Ariffen, 39, who has been reported missing since last week. Efforts to locate both individuals are currently being carried out by the police,” he said in a statement today.
He urged members of the public who have any information or who may have seen the two individuals to come forward to assist in the investigation by contacting the investigating officer, SM T. Kanggeswari, at 010-375 0353 or by reaching out to the nearest police station. — Bernama

Title: Higher Education Ministry urges industry to tap RM5m Public Private Research Network fund to boost productivity
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, April 1 — The Ministry of Higher Education Malaysia (MOHE) has called on industry players to take advantage of the RM5 million fund provided under the Public Private Research Network (PPRN) initiative to enhance company productivity.
The ministry’s Deputy Secretary-General (Policy) Datuk Dr Megat Sany Megat Ahmad Supian said the initiative, which began in 2015, has recorded the implementation of more than 700 innovation projects involving collaborations between public and private universities and industry players.
He said the initiative aims to cultivate knowledge transfer and expertise from researchers in higher education institutions (HEIs) through research collaborations with local companies that may lack expertise and capital.
“The government provides allocations whereby for small companies, we will fund up to 90 per cent of research costs, while the remaining 10 per cent is borne by the company itself.
“For medium-sized companies, the allocation may be shared on a 50-50 basis. What is important is that we want to cultivate a research culture within companies,” he said when appearing as a guest on the ‘Apa Khabar Malaysia’ programme broadcast by Bernama TV today.
Meanwhile, Megat Sany said the PPRN Conference themed ‘Academia-Industry Collaboration’ will be held at a hotel here on April 7 to promote best practices and showcase innovative products generated through the programme.
He said the conference, which is open to all, will also highlight and share success stories achieved through collaborations between higher education institutions and industry under the initiative, serving as inspiration for local companies.
Companies interested in participating in the PPRN initiative can obtain further information and apply via the epprn.mohe.gov.my website or the official PPRN Facebook page. — Bernama

Title: Police seek public help to find missing 15‑year‑old girl in Mukah
Article:
=MUKAH, April 1 — Police are appealing to the public to help locate a 15-year-old girl who went missing yesterday.
Mukah police chief DSP Muhamad Rizal Alias said Angel Daina from Jalan Tiong Hua here was last seen wearing a pink short-sleeved T-shirt and long black pants.
“She is described as having a slim build, approximately 155cm tall, fair-skinned, with an oval-shaped face and long hair,” he said in a statement.
Muhamad Rizal urged those who may have information on the teenager’s whereabouts to come forward and assist with the investigation.
“Anyone who knows her whereabouts is advised to contact the nearest police station or the Mukah police station on 084-871222,” he added. — The Borneo Post

Title: Malaysian vehicles among foreign cars required to have three‑quarters full tank to enter Brunei from April 1
Article:
MIRI, April 1 — Malaysian vehicle owners are advised to fill before entering Brunei, as its government will require all foreign vehicles to have at least three-quarters of a tank of fuel starting April 1.
Brunei’s Department of Energy (DOE) said the move is aimed at safeguarding the nation’s petroleum supply amid geopolitical developments in the Middle East since late February 2026.
“All foreign-registered vehicles entering Brunei Darussalam will be required to maintain a minimum fuel level of three-quarter tank upon entry.
“Foreign vehicles that do not comply with this requirement will not be allowed to enter Brunei Darussalam,” the statement said.
However, official government vehicles are exempted from this requirement.
Foreign vehicles in Brunei will only be allowed to purchase Shell V-Power Gasoline or Shell V-Power Diesel at participating petrol stations at prevailing market rates.
The rule does not apply to Brunei-registered vehicles returning from Malaysia on their first entry, but it will be enforced for second and subsequent entries within a 24-hour period.
The DOE has also instructed all fuel station operators to follow verification procedures strictly to prevent abuse.
“Actions will be taken for any attempts to use false or modified registration plates. Monitoring and enforcement will be actively carried out by the relevant authorities,” it said.
The department added that these measures are mandatory and form part of Brunei’s efforts to ensure subsidised fuel is used responsibly while protecting national energy security. — The Borneo Post

Title: Government withdraws appeal on Pastor Koh report; court awards RM15,000 costs to family
Article:
PUTRAJAYA, April 1 — The government today withdrew its appeal against a High Court ruling granting Pastor Raymond Koh’s family access to a classified report.
As reported by
Free Malaysia Today
(FMT), senior federal counsel Nurul Farhana Khalid informed the Court of Appeal that a notice of discontinuation had been filed, leading the panel to strike out the appeal.
“However, lawyer Michelle Wong objected, arguing that substantial work had gone into responding to the appeal.”
“She sought RM15,000 in costs, pointing out that an application to introduce fresh evidence was filed last month.”
The appellate court, chaired by Justice Supang Lian alongside Justices Ismail Brahim and K Muniandy, awarded RM15,000 in costs to Koh’s family despite the government’s request to waive it.

Title: Two teens drown during morning swim at Papar riverside campsite
Article:
PAPAR, April 1 — A recreational swim at a riverside campsite here ended in tragedy when two teenagers drowned at Pokdi Campsite in Mandalipau here yesterday morning.
Papar Fire and Rescue (Bomba) Station chief Asst Fire Supt Rozlan Osman said firefighters were dispatched to the location after receiving a distress call at 10.10am.
He said members of the public managed to pull both victims from the river before firefighters arrived at the scene.
“The victims were an 18-year-old boy and a 15-year-old girl,” Rozlan said in a statement.
Firefighters later brought the bodies from the riverbank and handed them over to the police for further action. — The Borneo Post

Title: Police shoot dead armed man who allegedly killed his younger brother in Tenom
Article:
TENOM, April 1 — A man was shot dead by police after he allegedly shot and killed his younger brother with a bakakuk (homemade shotgun) in an incident at Kampung Kangkawang at Jalan Kemabong here on Sunday.
Tenom District Police Chief Supt Azmir Abd Razak said the 57-year-old suspect died at the scene after police opened fire, striking him in the right arm, left chest and left rib.
His younger brother, 54, also died after sustaining eight gunshot wounds to his back in the 8am incident.
“Based on initial police investigation, the suspect, who had mental health problems, shot his younger brother after claiming to have heard that his sibling and sister-in-law were talking badly about him,” Azmir said in a statement.
Police were alerted to the incident and a team of officers was dispatched to the scene.
Upon arrival, villagers informed the police that the suspect was inside a house armed with a firearm and threatening to shoot anyone who approached the area.
Police then surrounded the house and ordered the suspect to surrender three times, but he refused to comply.
Several tear gas canisters were fired into the house in an attempt to force him out, but the suspect still did not surrender.
Azmir said the situation escalated when the suspect ran towards the kitchen while pointing his weapon at a police officer standing near a window.
“In self-defence, police fired three shots that hit the suspect in the right arm, left chest and left rib.
“The suspect then collapsed on the kitchen floor before being confirmed dead at the scene,” he said.
Inspection at the scene found that the suspect’s bakakuk still contained a live round in the chamber.
Police have seized several items including the bakakuk firearm, ammunition, spent casings and a long machete at the scene of the incident.
The case is being investigated under Section 302 of the Penal Code for murder and Section 307 of the Penal Code for attempted murder.
“Further investigation is still ongoing,” Azmir said. — The Borneo Post

Title: Flight costs covered: MACC tells James Chai to return in RM1.1b ARM probe
Article:
PUTRAJAYA, April 1 — The Malaysian Anti‑Corruption Commission (MACC) has offered to pay for political analyst James Chai’s flight back to Malaysia as part of its investigation into alleged misconduct tied to a government‑backed semiconductor project worth RM1.11 billion with UK‑based ARM Holdings.
MACC said the probe is “actively ongoing”, with statements already taken from 17 witnesses including Economy Ministry officers and Malaysian Investment Development Authority (Mida) officials. Key documents have also been secured, and another eight witnesses are expected to be called, some of them overseas.
Chai Jin Shern, better known as James Chai, has been identified as a required witness. A notice to locate him was issued on March 4, but his lawyers later claimed he could not attend due to travel costs and work commitments. MACC granted him an extension until March 31, yet he failed to respond.
In a fresh letter today, MACC gave him two weeks to appear and said it would bear the cost of his return flight.
The commission warned that if he continues to ignore its requests, despite being granted two extensions, it may seek international cooperation through Interpol or foreign enforcement agencies.
MACC stressed that all individuals called to assist investigations are obliged to cooperate. “If James Chai maintains that he has committed no wrongdoing, the appropriate course of action is to present himself to MACC to provide a statement, rather than issuing explanations in the public domain,” it said.
The commission reiterated that no one is above the law and pledged to conduct the investigation professionally, independently, and based on evidence.

Title: Ramanan: Two seasoned professionals join Skills Development Fund Corp board to drive skills‑training governance
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, April 1 — Human Resources Minister Datuk Seri R. Ramanan announced that the appointment of two experienced individuals to the Board of Directors (BOD) of the Skills Development Fund Corporation (PTPK) is part of an effort to strengthen the nation’s human capital development through more strategic and high-impact skills training.
The appointees are Ramelle Ashram Ramli, who will serve as a board member as well as deputy chairman, and Ahsim Jamat, who has been appointed as a board member.
Ramanan said that the appointments are in line with the government’s commitment to ensure that PTPK continues to play a central role as a key institution in supporting the national skills training ecosystem, including expanding access to training financing and enhancing the employability of graduates from Technical and Vocational Education and Training (TVET).
“These appointments also reflect the government’s emphasis on leadership grounded in industry experience and practical expertise, in efforts to strengthen governance and the efficiency of institutions related to skills training,” he said in a statement today.
